title: What an asset class is and why the distinction matters
subtitle: Every investment belongs to a category. Understanding those categories is the prerequisite for every decision that follows.

body.content: An asset class is a group of investments that share similar characteristics — how they generate returns, how they're legally structured, and how they tend to behave when the economy changes. Not a vague category. A real distinction with real consequences for how you build a portfolio.

items: Equities (stocks): fractional ownership of businesses. Returns come from earnings growth and dividends. Highest long-term returns, highest short-term volatility.
Fixed income (bonds): loans to governments or companies. Returns come from interest payments. Lower returns than stocks over long periods, lower volatility.
Cash equivalents: money market funds, T-bills, CDs, savings accounts. Minimal return. Maximum liquidity and safety.
Real assets: real estate, commodities, infrastructure. Returns from income or physical scarcity. Good inflation protection.
Alternatives: private equity, hedge funds, venture capital. Higher potential returns, lower liquidity, high minimums. Mostly inaccessible to regular investors.

body.content: This isn't just taxonomy. These categories describe different legal structures, different return drivers, and different behaviors under the same economic conditions. That's what makes the distinction matter.
Why the legal structure actually matters

When you own a stock, you're an owner of the business. Last in line if it fails — but you capture all the upside. When you own a bond, you're a creditor. First in line to be paid, but capped at your coupon rate. These are fundamentally different legal claims, and they produce completely different payoff profiles.

body.label: Real talk
body.content: In a bankruptcy, creditors (bondholders) get paid before equity holders (stockholders). Full stop. This is why bonds are called 'safer' for a given company — the legal claim is senior. But flip it around: if the company 10x's, bondholders still just get their coupon. Stockholders own all of that upside. You're choosing between first claim on a fixed amount or last claim on an unlimited one. Neither is wrong — they just serve different purposes.

Stocks do well when the economy is growing and corporate earnings are strong. Bonds hold steady or rally when growth slows and interest rates fall. Commodities and real assets tend to outperform when inflation is high. Cash actually earns something meaningful when rates are elevated and everything else is falling.

No single asset class wins in every environment. That's not a problem — it's the entire reason to own more than one. The goal isn't to predict which will win next year. Nobody does that reliably. The goal is to understand what you own well enough to build something that doesn't all fail at once.

body.label: Key concept
body.content: 2022 is the interesting exception. Stocks fell hard. Bonds — which normally cushion stock losses — also fell hard, because inflation hit both simultaneously. That was unusual. It's a reminder that no relationship holds forever, and you need to understand the 'why' behind correlations, not just the historical pattern.

Most people build portfolios without being able to articulate what they actually own or why. They have a collection of things that felt smart at the time. That's not a portfolio — it's a list. Understanding asset classes is step one of building something intentional.

items: Know the legal structure: are you an owner or a creditor?
Know the return driver: earnings growth, interest income, physical scarcity, or price changes?
Know the economic sensitivity: what conditions help this asset, and what hurts it?
Know the correlation: does it tend to move with or against what you already own?

body.content: Risk comes from not knowing what you're doing. — Warren Buffett

That applies to asset classes more than almost anything. You can own a dozen things and still not understand your portfolio if you don't know what each one actually is, why it behaves the way it does, and what economic conditions it's built for.

An asset class is a group of investments with the same legal structure, return driver, and economic behavior. Stocks are ownership. Bonds are lending. Real assets are physical claims. Cash is a temporary parking spot. Understanding the categories is the foundation for every portfolio decision that follows.
